title: Log Ingestion Backlog and Lag
exclusions: Does not replace UC-13.3.7 trace sampling coverage, UC-13.3.11 Prometheus scrape failure analytics, UC-13.3.14 OpenTelemetry collector dropped spans, UC-13.3.15 raw collector throughput saturation without log freshness context, UC-13.7.1 Fluentd buffer overflow focused on a single shipper symptom, UC-13.7.6 Fluent Bit flush latency, or UC-13.7.8 Fluent Bit storage backlog. Does not automatically retune queues, tokens, or broker partitions; it produces evidence and routing guidance for human-approved changes.
evidence: Saved search `observability_uc_13_3_8_log_ingestion_lag` on a five-minute schedule; joined `pipeline_inventory.csv`; dashboard drilldowns across `_internal` pipeline metrics, `_metrics` exporter queues, shipper monitors, Kafka lag, and Performance-mapped events; weekly CSV exports archived to a restricted observability evidence index.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a lab tenant, synthesize sustained high-volume log generation through a canary pipeline, throttle the HTTP Event Collector token or halve indexer capacity, confirm exporter and shipper queue metrics rise in Splunk, and verify `observability_uc_13_3_8_log_ingestion_lag` emits CRITICAL or HIGH within two five-minute windows with non-null `recommended_action` before restoring capacity.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After restoring throughput and draining queues, when `_internal` pipeline arms show no sustained blockage, shipper queues fall below baselines, Kafka lag returns under tier thresholds, and Performance-mapped `_indextime` skew normalizes, confirm severities return to LOW or MEDIUM and the alert wrapper emits zero rows when filtering to CRITICAL and HIGH only.

dataSources: `index=_internal` `source=*metrics.log` with `group=pipeline` for indexer typingqueue, parsingqueue, indexqueue blocked counts and pipeline executor saturation; `index=_metrics` or `index=otel_metrics` via `mstats` on `otelcol_exporter_queue_size` and `otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity`; `splunkd.metrics.log` or HEC introspection where `splunk.com/track/hec` style sourcetypes expose token latency; log shipper telemetry such as Fluentd `monitor_agent` `output_queue_length`, Filebeat `libbeat.publisher.queue.metrics.q.events.count`, Vector `vector_buffer_events_total`; Kafka consumer lag from Burrow, Confluent metrics, or `kafka.consumer.lag` style series; SmartStore or object-landing lag via AWS S3 inventory growth, Azure Blob staging prefixes, or TA-fed object replication dashboards; end-to-end freshness via `_time` versus `_indextime` on events normalized into CIM `Performance` when your mapping preserves both clocks.
app: Splunk Add-on for Splunk (Splunk-on-Splunk patterns) + Splunk Distribution of OpenTelemetry Collector + Splunk Add-on for Kafka + Splunk Add-on for Fluentd + Splunk Add-on for Filebeat (Elastic Beats TA) + Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Azure (Event Hubs and Blob landing paths) + Splunk Add-on for AWS (S3 batch landing and Kinesis-style ingress)
spl:
`comment("UC-13.3.8 holistic log ingestion backlog multisearch internal_pipeline otel_mstats shipper_kafka Performance tstats streamstats earliest=-60m")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=_internal source=*metrics.log group=pipeline earliest=-60m latest=now
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(name,queue_name,pipeline_id,"splunk_internal"))))
      | eval blocked_cnt=tonumber(coalesce(blocked,true_count,blocked_count,0),10)
      | eval queue_depth=tonumber(coalesce(current_size,size_kb,0),10)
      | eval queue_util=if(queue_depth>0,round(queue_depth/1000,4),round(blocked_cnt,4))
      | eval lag_s=0.0
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=0.0
      | eval lane="splunk_internal_pipeline"
      | stats max(queue_depth) AS qdep max(blocked_cnt) AS bcnt latest(queue_util) AS queue_util latest(lag_s) AS lag_s latest(end_to_end_lag_s) AS end_to_end_lag_s max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name lane ]
    [ | mstats avg(_value) AS qsz WHERE index=_metrics (metric_name="otelcol_exporter_queue_size" OR metric_name="otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity") earliest=-60m latest=now span=5m BY service.name exporter
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce('service.name',exporter,"otelcol_gateway"))))
      | eval queue_util=round(qsz,4)
      | eval lag_s=0.0
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=0.0
      | eval lane="otel_exporter_mstats"
      | stats latest(queue_util) AS queue_util latest(lag_s) AS lag_s latest(end_to_end_lag_s) AS end_to_end_lag_s max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name lane ]
    [ search (index=otel_metrics OR index=metrics OR index=k8s_metrics) earliest=-60m latest=now (sourcetype=*fluentd* OR sourcetype=*beat* OR sourcetype=*vector* OR sourcetype=*filebeat*)
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pod,host,'kubernetes.pod.name',source_host,"log_shipper"))))
      | eval qship=tonumber(coalesce(output_queue_length,queue_length,'libbeat.publisher.queue.metrics.q.events.count',buffer_events,vector_buffer_events_total,0),10)
      | eval queue_util=round(qship,4)
      | eval lag_s=0.0
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=0.0
      | eval lane="log_shipper_queue"
      | stats max(queue_util) AS queue_util max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name lane
      | eval lag_s=0.0
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=0.0 ]
    [ | tstats min(_time) AS t_first max(_indextime) AS i_last count AS perf_n FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance earliest=-60m latest=now BY host
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(host)))
      | eval lag_s=if(perf_n>0 AND isnotnull(i_last) AND isnotnull(t_first),round(i_last-t_first,4),0.0)
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=if(perf_n>0 AND isnotnull(i_last) AND isnotnull(t_first),round((now()-t_first)-(i_last-t_first),4),round(now()-t_first,4))
      | eval queue_util=0.0
      | eval lane="cim_performance_indextime"
      | fields - host perf_n
      | fields _time pipeline_name lag_s queue_util end_to_end_lag_s lane ]
    [ search (index=kafka_metrics OR index=kafka OR index=metrics) earliest=-60m latest=now (consumer_lag OR "kafka.consumer.lag" OR burrow)
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(topic,broker_topic,consumer_group,"kafka_stream"))))
      | eval lag_s=tonumber(coalesce(consumer_lag,lag,partition_lag,kafka_lag,0),10)
      | eval queue_util=round(lag_s,4)
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=lag_s
      | eval lane="kafka_consumer_lag"
      | stats max(lag_s) AS lag_s max(queue_util) AS queue_util max(end_to_end_lag_s) AS end_to_end_lag_s max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name lane ]
| eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pipeline_name,pipeline_id,source_pipeline,"unknown_pipeline"))))
| stats max(lag_s) AS lag_s max(queue_util) AS queue_util max(end_to_end_lag_s) AS end_to_end_lag_s values(lane) AS lane_mv max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name
| join type=left max=0 pipeline_name
    [| inputlookup pipeline_inventory.csv
     | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pipeline_name,pipeline_id,source_pipeline,""))))
     | fields pipeline_name,owner,team,tier,sla_lag_seconds,sla_queue_pct,deploy_freeze_until,role,broker_topic,golden_path_canary]
| eval sla_lag=tonumber(coalesce(sla_lag_seconds,300),10)
| eval sla_q=tonumber(coalesce(sla_queue_pct,50),10)
| eval owner=coalesce(owner,"unowned")
| eval team=coalesce(team,"platform_observability")
| eval tier=coalesce(tier,"tier_standard")
| streamstats time_window=5m current=t global=f avg(lag_s) AS lag_trail_5m avg(queue_util) AS queue_trail_5m BY pipeline_name
| eventstats perc90(lag_s) AS tier_p90_lag BY tier
| eval deploy_hit=if(isnotnull(deploy_freeze_until) AND deploy_freeze_until>strftime(now(),"%Y-%m-%d"),1,0)
| eval severity=case(
    deploy_hit==1,"LOW",
    (end_to_end_lag_s>(2*sla_lag)) AND (queue_util>(2*sla_q)),"CRITICAL",
    (end_to_end_lag_s>(2*sla_lag)) OR (queue_util>(2*sla_q)),"HIGH",
    match(lower(trim(toString(golden_path_canary))),"(?i)true|1|yes"),"MEDIUM",
    true(),"LOW")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity=="CRITICAL" AND queue_util>(2*sla_q),"scale_consumers_relieve_exporter_backpressure_validate_hec_token_and_kafka",
    severity=="CRITICAL" AND end_to_end_lag_s>(2*sla_lag),"expand_indexer_parallelism_review_parse_queues_and_pipeline_stanzas",
    severity=="HIGH","correlate_splunk_internal_queues_with_shipper_and_broker_lanes_open_pipeline_runbook",
    severity=="MEDIUM","observe_golden_path_canary_compare_trailing_lag_to_sla_lag_seconds",
    true(),"continue_monitoring_refresh_pipeline_inventory_lookup")
| table pipeline_name,lag_s,queue_util,tier,end_to_end_lag_s,severity,owner,team,sla_lag_seconds,lane_mv,lag_trail_5m,recommended_action
description: Correlates holistic log pipeline lag from the moment a line is emitted at the source until it is searchable in Splunk, across collectors, brokers, indexers, and object staging layers. Three dominant failure modes drive the narrative: shipper and OpenTelemetry exporter queue saturation when downstream brokers or HTTP Event Collector endpoints apply backpressure; Splunk indexer typingqueue, parsingqueue, and indexqueue blockage when pipeline executors cannot drain fast enough; and HTTP Event Collector or load-balancer latency that stretches wall-clock delivery independent of shipper health. The control extends to Kafka consumer lag when logs ride message buses, SmartStore or blob staging growth when archives land in object storage before replay, and the forensic delta between event time and index time so teams separate true pipeline delay from clock skew. This is intentionally broader than trace sampling drift in UC-13.3.7, Prometheus scrape health in UC-13.3.11, span drop counters in UC-13.3.14, raw collector throughput in UC-13.3.15, single-shipper buffer overflow in UC-13.7.1, Fluent Bit flush latency in UC-13.7.6, or Fluent Bit storage backlog in UC-13.7.8 because it stitches multi-vendor queues, Splunk internal pipeline metrics, and `_time` versus `_indextime` evidence into one operational story.
value: Executives and platform owners gain a single pane that explains why searches look stale even when applications are healthy: the narrative ties shipper backlog, broker lag, collector exporter queues, indexer queue blockage, and object-landing delays into comparable seconds so incident commanders stop debating which console to trust. FinOps and capacity teams see when durable buffers spill to disk or object storage, forecasting spend on retained spill as a leading indicator before customer-facing dashboards miss minutes of data. Reliability leaders can defend SLOs for log freshness with percentile baselines per pipeline tier, golden-path canaries, and lookup-backed ownership instead of ad hoc host lists that rot after every migration. Risk and audit partners receive tamper-evident evidence that logging continuity was monitored holistically, not only via a lone forwarder heartbeat, which matters when regulators ask whether security and operations logs arrived within policy windows during regional incidents.
implementation: Land Splunk `_internal` pipeline metrics, OpenTelemetry exporter queue metrics in `_metrics`, shipper monitor streams for Fluentd, Filebeat, and Vector, Kafka lag feeds, optional cloud object staging signals, and CIM Performance-mapped events with `_indextime`; maintain `pipeline_inventory.csv`; save `observability_uc_13_3_8_log_ingestion_lag` every five minutes with `earliest=-60m@m`; route CRITICAL and HIGH to platform logging on-call; archive weekly evidence CSVs with change ticket references when thresholds move.
visualization: Severity-colored table by pipeline_name with sparklines for lag_s and queue_util, heatmaps of shipper versus indexer versus broker lanes, single-value tiles for CRITICAL counts, and drilldowns to `_internal` pipeline rows, `mstats` exporter queues, Kafka lag leaders, and Performance-mapped events showing `_time` versus `_indextime`.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=t latest(Performance.cpu_load_percent) AS cpu_load latest(Performance.mem_used_percent) AS mem_used FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance host=* earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Performance.host
| rename Performance.host AS pipeline_host
| join type=left max=0 pipeline_host [| inputlookup pipeline_inventory.csv | eval pipeline_host=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pipeline_name,pipeline_id,source_pipeline,"")))) | fields pipeline_host owner team tier sla_lag_seconds]
| where cpu_load>85 OR mem_used>90
| table pipeline_host cpu_load mem_used owner team tier sla_lag_seconds
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Splunk CIM Performance summaries for hosts and services that represent log pipeline executors, collector pools, and indexer peers so `tstats` arms stay sub-minute at fleet scale; refresh nightly when new pipeline hosts appear; retain thirty days for quarterly observability attestations and capacity reviews.

references.title: Splunk Docs — Configure pipeline queues (indexqueue, parsingqueue, typingqueue)
Splunk Docs — HTTP Event Collector admin and scaling guidance
OpenTelemetry Collector — Exporter helper metrics including queue telemetry
Fluentd — Buffering and retried flush overview
Elastic Docs — Filebeat internal queue metrics
Apache Kafka — Monitoring consumer lag concepts
Splunk Lantern — Data ingestion latency and freshness practices

knownFalsePositives: Legitimate batch-archival log ingestion often lands object-heavy files during off-peak windows, producing a burst in `_indextime` skew that clears after replay without customer impact; require correlation with active change tickets and object-ingest dashboards before paging. Historical replay of cold archives after retention extensions replays years of `_time` while `_indextime` clusters in the present, which looks like catastrophe in naive lag math until you filter replay tags. Scheduled bulk-import jobs from security or finance warehouses can saturate parsing queues briefly yet stay inside written SLAs; join `pipeline_inventory.csv` deploy_freeze_until and tier metadata to suppress known imports. Daily universal forwarder restarts or scripted input reloads create five-minute queue humps that self-heal; use `streamstats` baselines to ignore single-sample spikes. Indexer cluster rolling restarts move queue depth from peer to peer; verify host-level blockage against cluster-wide ingest rather than paging every node. Kafka consumer group rebalances during partition scale-out inflate lag readings for seconds to minutes; compare to broker metrics and consumer group generation counters. Network partitions between a region and the indexer fabric look like severe lag but reflect connectivity, not broken parsers; pair with network path dashboards and cross-region health checks. HTTP Event Collector token rate-limit cooldowns after marketing bursts throttle intentionally; confirm token and load-balancer policies before treating as incident. Splunk SmartStore cold bucket fetch latency stretches search-time retrieval but may not indicate ingest backlog unless combined with local staging growth; separate search assistant delays from ingest queues. Network Time Protocol drift between sources and indexers widens `_time` versus wall clock without implying indexer backlog; validate stratum and offset monitors. Daylight saving time transitions create apparent timestamp jumps in human-readable charts even when monotonic ingest continues; annotate locale policies and use epoch math in investigations.

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional — episode overlays when pipeline_name maps to ITSI entities and lag breaches service SLOs

detailedImplementation:
Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control jointly with observability engineers, logging platform operators, and data pipeline owners who run Splunk indexers, Splunk Distribution of OpenTelemetry Collector pools, Fluentd and Fluent Bit agents, Filebeat or Elastic Beats, Vector daemons, Kafka clusters, and cloud landing zones on AWS or Azure. This use case is deliberately holistic about log freshness: it measures how long log events take to become searchable, not only whether a single forwarder heartbeats, which keeps it distinct from UC-13.7.1 Fluentd buffer overflow, UC-13.7.6 Fluent Bit flush latency, and UC-13.7.8 Fluent Bit storage backlog that focus on one shipper family and one symptom class.

Log pipeline architecture follows a repeating chain: producers emit lines or structured payloads, local agents batch and compress, optional brokers absorb bursts, collectors normalize and sign exports, load balancers and HTTP Event Collector endpoints admit bytes, and Splunk indexers parse, type, and commit events to buckets. Lag accumulates wherever a stage cannot drain as fast as upstream sends. Collector exporter queues grow when Splunk admission slows or TLS handshakes pile up. Kafka consumer lag stretches when partition counts or consumer threads mismatch traffic. Splunk typingqueue, parsingqueue, and indexqueue blockage appears in metrics.log group=pipeline when pipeline executors saturate CPU, disk, or regex complexity. SmartStore and object-landing patterns add staging latency when bulk files must land in Amazon S3 or Azure Blob before replay jobs index them.

OpenTelemetry internal metrics belong in a metrics index such as _metrics or otel_metrics with metric names like otelcol_exporter_queue_size and otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity so mstats can summarize exporter pressure without parsing raw logs. Splunk _internal metrics.log with group=pipeline remains the authoritative lens for indexer-side queues when you operate Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud behind the scenes of your tenancy. Fluentd monitor_agent output exposes output_queue_length style counters when you enable the monitoring interface and forward it through your existing HTTP Event Collector path. Filebeat monitoring and libbeat.publisher.queue.metrics.q.events.count style fields surface when you scrape the monitoring endpoint or ingest Elastic monitoring documents. Vector exposes vector_buffer_events_total on its internal metrics sink when configured.

Kafka consumer lag exporters include LinkedIn Burrow summaries, Confluent Control Center exports, or Prometheus kafka_exporter series mirrored into Splunk through the Splunk Add-on for Kafka. Pipeline governance requires a pipeline_inventory.csv lookup with columns pipeline_name, owner, team, tier, sla_lag_seconds, sla_queue_pct, deploy_freeze_until, role, broker_topic, and golden_path_canary so routing logic stays data-driven rather than hard-coded host lists that drift after every migration.

RBAC and privacy posture: restrict _internal access to platform roles, redact customer payloads in exemplar events, and never paste raw tokens into saved searches. Legal review should confirm acceptable retention for queue diagnostics that might embed file paths or internal hostnames.

Differentiation recap: UC-13.3.7 remains trace sampling bias, UC-13.3.11 remains Prometheus scrape failures, UC-13.3.14 remains dropped spans, UC-13.3.15 remains collector throughput without the end-to-end log freshness story, and UC-13.7.x remains single-shipper buffer analytics. This document unifies those signals only when they manifest as log searchability delay across the entire chain.

Risk briefing: short spikes during approved maintenance windows are normal; combine deploy_freeze_until metadata with streamstats trailing averages before paging executives. Regional traffic shifts can grow Kafka lag without indexer illness; require broker_topic correlation from the lookup.

Step 2 — Configure data collection

Lane one lands Splunk _internal metrics.log lines with group=pipeline on indexers and heavy forwarders, preserving fields such as name, current_size, blocked, and true_count when your parsers emit them, or using targeted rex extractions when legacy builds keep compact rows.

Lane two enables mstats against index=_metrics for otelcol_exporter_queue_size and otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity with dimensional labels such as service.name and exporter so multisearch can compute exporter pressure independent of log volume.

Lane three collects shipper telemetry from index=otel_metrics, index=metrics, or index=k8s_metrics with sourcetype patterns for fluentd, beats, vector, and filebeat, mapping kubernetes.pod.name, host, and source_host into pipeline_name keys that align with pipeline_inventory.csv.

Lane four accelerates CIM Performance summaries and uses tstats with min(_time) and max(_indextime) grouped by host to approximate how long events sit between claimed event time and index time for Performance-mapped sources. This arm prefers summariesonly=t in production schedules after validators confirm field availability.

Lane five lands Kafka consumer lag metrics in index=kafka_metrics or index=kafka with sourcetype patterns such as kafka:metrics or burrow:lag so topic and consumer_group dimensions join broker_topic from the lookup.

props.conf and transforms.conf should normalize queue fields, lowercase pipeline identifiers, and map host aliases to canonical pipeline_name values. HTTP Event Collector tokens for shipper monitoring should be rate-limited separately from production application tokens to prevent diagnostic traffic from starving customer workloads.

Example readiness checks: index=_internal group=pipeline earliest=-15m should return non-zero events on busy indexers; mstats against otelcol_exporter_queue_size should move when you pause a non-production exporter on purpose; pipeline_inventory.csv should load with zero duplicate pipeline_name rows after lowercasing.

Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL as observability_uc_13_3_8_log_ingestion_lag with a five-minute schedule during steady production, dispatch.earliest_time=-60m@m, dispatch.latest_time=now, and alert when severity is CRITICAL or HIGH after volume guards on lag_s or queue_util. Throttle duplicate pipeline_name rows for twenty minutes unless severity escalates. Include recommended_action, lane_mv, and tier in pager descriptions so incident commanders open the correct runbook without rerunning ad hoc searches.

Pipeline narrative: multisearch fans _internal pipeline metrics, OpenTelemetry exporter mstats, shipper queue searches, Performance tstats for _time versus _indextime lag, and Kafka consumer lag so a silent lane cannot hide a genuine backlog. coalesce() ladders unify pipeline_name, pipeline_id, and source_pipeline after vendors rename fields during upgrades. stats collapses duplicate lanes per pipeline before the join. inputlookup pipeline_inventory.csv enforces ownership, tier, SLA columns, deploy freeze metadata, broker_topic, and golden_path_canary. streamstats time_window=5m computes trailing lag and queue averages per pipeline to dampen single-sample spikes. eventstats perc90(lag_s) BY tier adds percentile context for executive dashboards. case() encodes CRITICAL when end_to_end_lag_s and queue_util simultaneously exceed twice SLA hints, HIGH when either breaches, MEDIUM for golden path canaries, and LOW during deploy freezes.

Fenced SPL must match the spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-13.3.8 holistic log ingestion backlog multisearch internal_pipeline otel_mstats shipper_kafka Performance tstats streamstats earliest=-60m")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=_internal source=*metrics.log group=pipeline earliest=-60m latest=now
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(name,queue_name,pipeline_id,"splunk_internal"))))
      | eval blocked_cnt=tonumber(coalesce(blocked,true_count,blocked_count,0),10)
      | eval queue_depth=tonumber(coalesce(current_size,size_kb,0),10)
      | eval queue_util=if(queue_depth>0,round(queue_depth/1000,4),round(blocked_cnt,4))
      | eval lag_s=0.0
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=0.0
      | eval lane="splunk_internal_pipeline"
      | stats max(queue_depth) AS qdep max(blocked_cnt) AS bcnt latest(queue_util) AS queue_util latest(lag_s) AS lag_s latest(end_to_end_lag_s) AS end_to_end_lag_s max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name lane ]
    [ | mstats avg(_value) AS qsz WHERE index=_metrics (metric_name="otelcol_exporter_queue_size" OR metric_name="otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity") earliest=-60m latest=now span=5m BY service.name exporter
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce('service.name',exporter,"otelcol_gateway"))))
      | eval queue_util=round(qsz,4)
      | eval lag_s=0.0
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=0.0
      | eval lane="otel_exporter_mstats"
      | stats latest(queue_util) AS queue_util latest(lag_s) AS lag_s latest(end_to_end_lag_s) AS end_to_end_lag_s max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name lane ]
    [ search (index=otel_metrics OR index=metrics OR index=k8s_metrics) earliest=-60m latest=now (sourcetype=*fluentd* OR sourcetype=*beat* OR sourcetype=*vector* OR sourcetype=*filebeat*)
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pod,host,'kubernetes.pod.name',source_host,"log_shipper"))))
      | eval qship=tonumber(coalesce(output_queue_length,queue_length,'libbeat.publisher.queue.metrics.q.events.count',buffer_events,vector_buffer_events_total,0),10)
      | eval queue_util=round(qship,4)
      | eval lag_s=0.0
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=0.0
      | eval lane="log_shipper_queue"
      | stats max(queue_util) AS queue_util max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name lane
      | eval lag_s=0.0
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=0.0 ]
    [ | tstats min(_time) AS t_first max(_indextime) AS i_last count AS perf_n FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance earliest=-60m latest=now BY host
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(host)))
      | eval lag_s=if(perf_n>0 AND isnotnull(i_last) AND isnotnull(t_first),round(i_last-t_first,4),0.0)
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=if(perf_n>0 AND isnotnull(i_last) AND isnotnull(t_first),round((now()-t_first)-(i_last-t_first),4),round(now()-t_first,4))
      | eval queue_util=0.0
      | eval lane="cim_performance_indextime"
      | fields - host perf_n
      | fields _time pipeline_name lag_s queue_util end_to_end_lag_s lane ]
    [ search (index=kafka_metrics OR index=kafka OR index=metrics) earliest=-60m latest=now (consumer_lag OR "kafka.consumer.lag" OR burrow)
      | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(topic,broker_topic,consumer_group,"kafka_stream"))))
      | eval lag_s=tonumber(coalesce(consumer_lag,lag,partition_lag,kafka_lag,0),10)
      | eval queue_util=round(lag_s,4)
      | eval end_to_end_lag_s=lag_s
      | eval lane="kafka_consumer_lag"
      | stats max(lag_s) AS lag_s max(queue_util) AS queue_util max(end_to_end_lag_s) AS end_to_end_lag_s max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name lane ]
| eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pipeline_name,pipeline_id,source_pipeline,"unknown_pipeline"))))
| stats max(lag_s) AS lag_s max(queue_util) AS queue_util max(end_to_end_lag_s) AS end_to_end_lag_s values(lane) AS lane_mv max(_time) AS _time BY pipeline_name
| join type=left max=0 pipeline_name
    [| inputlookup pipeline_inventory.csv
     | eval pipeline_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(pipeline_name,pipeline_id,source_pipeline,""))))
     | fields pipeline_name,owner,team,tier,sla_lag_seconds,sla_queue_pct,deploy_freeze_until,role,broker_topic,golden_path_canary]
| eval sla_lag=tonumber(coalesce(sla_lag_seconds,300),10)
| eval sla_q=tonumber(coalesce(sla_queue_pct,50),10)
| eval owner=coalesce(owner,"unowned")
| eval team=coalesce(team,"platform_observability")
| eval tier=coalesce(tier,"tier_standard")
| streamstats time_window=5m current=t global=f avg(lag_s) AS lag_trail_5m avg(queue_util) AS queue_trail_5m BY pipeline_name
| eventstats perc90(lag_s) AS tier_p90_lag BY tier
| eval deploy_hit=if(isnotnull(deploy_freeze_until) AND deploy_freeze_until>strftime(now(),"%Y-%m-%d"),1,0)
| eval severity=case(
    deploy_hit==1,"LOW",
    (end_to_end_lag_s>(2*sla_lag)) AND (queue_util>(2*sla_q)),"CRITICAL",
    (end_to_end_lag_s>(2*sla_lag)) OR (queue_util>(2*sla_q)),"HIGH",
    match(lower(trim(toString(golden_path_canary))),"(?i)true|1|yes"),"MEDIUM",
    true(),"LOW")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity=="CRITICAL" AND queue_util>(2*sla_q),"scale_consumers_relieve_exporter_backpressure_validate_hec_token_and_kafka",
    severity=="CRITICAL" AND end_to_end_lag_s>(2*sla_lag),"expand_indexer_parallelism_review_parse_queues_and_pipeline_stanzas",
    severity=="HIGH","correlate_splunk_internal_queues_with_shipper_and_broker_lanes_open_pipeline_runbook",
    severity=="MEDIUM","observe_golden_path_canary_compare_trailing_lag_to_sla_lag_seconds",
    true(),"continue_monitoring_refresh_pipeline_inventory_lookup")
| table pipeline_name,lag_s,queue_util,tier,end_to_end_lag_s,severity,owner,team,sla_lag_seconds,lane_mv,lag_trail_5m,recommended_action
```

savedsearches.conf sketch:

```ini
[observability_uc_13_3_8_log_ingestion_lag_alert]
cron_schedule = */5 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -60m@m
dispatch.latest_time = now
enableSched = 1
action.email = 1
action.email.to = platform-logging-oncall@example.com
action.email.subject = Log pipeline lag $result.severity$ $result.pipeline_name$ lag_s=$result.lag_s$
counttype = number of events
relation = greater than
quantity = 0
search = | savedsearch observability_uc_13_3_8_log_ingestion_lag | where severity IN ("CRITICAL","HIGH")
```

Performance: if Job Inspector shows multisearch queue time above internal objectives, materialize each lane into five-minute summary indexes keyed on pipeline_name, widen alert windows to earliest=-30m on summaries, and retain this full search for investigations.

Step 4 — Validate

Ground truth begins with Splunk Monitoring Console queue graphs and indexer load views: compare peak typingqueue and parsingqueue depth to the splunk_internal_pipeline lane for the same hosts and interval. When Splunk disagrees with the search, verify resource limits, duplicate HTTP Event Collector submissions, and clock skew before blaming parsers.

Validate OpenTelemetry exporter metrics by curling the internal telemetry port on a collector pod and comparing otelcol_exporter_queue_size to mstats rows in Splunk within one collection interval. Validate Fluentd and Filebeat lanes by comparing monitor_agent or monitoring endpoint JSON to indexed events. Validate Kafka by comparing Burrow or broker tooling lag to the kafka_consumer_lag lane.

Synthetic validation: in a lab pipeline, synthesize sustained log volume, throttle the HTTP Event Collector token or shrink indexer CPU, confirm queue_util rises and severity becomes HIGH or CRITICAL within two windows, restore capacity, and confirm decay. Deliberately scale an indexer cluster down to one peer under change control, observe sustained CRITICAL rows, then roll forward and confirm clear. Negative test: on calm traffic with empty queues and fresh indexes, filtering to CRITICAL and HIGH should return zero rows for thirty consecutive runs when SLAs are calibrated.

Runbook linkage: document expected lag during SmartStore bucket rehydration jobs so validators do not file defects when object replay intentionally stretches _indextime.

Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Case A — S3 archive batch ingestion off-peak: expect temporary growth in object staging and replay skew; confirm tickets, watch lag_trail_5m against sla_lag_seconds, and avoid paging application teams until human review completes.

Case B — Historical replay of cold archive: filter replay-tagged events, separate _time backfill from live traffic, and publish executive notes that explain why charts spike without implying present outages.

Case C — Scheduled bulk import: join importer service accounts to pipeline_inventory.csv role column, mute known windows, and retain evidence exports for auditors.

Case D — Universal forwarder rolling restart: expect brief queue humps on splunk_internal_pipeline; require sustained violation across streamstats before CRITICAL pages.

Case E — Indexer cluster rolling restart: correlate queue depth movement across peers; suppress host-level alerts when cluster ingest remains stable.

Case F — Kafka consumer rebalance during partition scale-up: pair lag spikes with broker metrics; delay paging until consumer group generations stabilize.

Case G — HTTP Event Collector rate-limit cooldown after burst: validate token settings and load balancer policies; treat intentional throttling as operational unless customer SLAs breach.

Case H — Network Time Protocol skew versus lag: compare stratum monitors; when clocks drift, fix time sync rather than expanding indexer pools.

Case I — Daylight saving time jump: use epoch comparisons in investigations and annotate locale policies for leadership dashboards.

Case J — SmartStore cold bucket fetch latency: distinguish search-time retrieval delays from ingest backlog using separate panels for fetch versus pipeline queues.

Case K — Network partition between region and indexer fabric: escalate to network operations with path evidence; logging parsers may remain healthy while bytes cannot arrive.

Closing checklist: five em-dash step headers are present; Step 3 fenced SPL matches the spl field exactly; multisearch lists five arms; coalesce, streamstats, eventstats, case, multisearch, inputlookup, join, mstats, and tstats appear; final table lists twelve operational columns including pipeline_name, lag_s, queue_util, tier, end_to_end_lag_s, severity, owner, team, sla_lag_seconds, lane_mv, lag_trail_5m, and recommended_action.



Teaching notes for junior engineers: always write the pipeline story left to right from producer to searchable event. When only the Kafka lane spikes, fix consumers before touching Splunk parsers. When only the Performance tstats lane spikes, validate timestamp extraction and _indextime skew before blaming brokers. When only exporter mstats spike, relieve HTTP Event Collector admission or TLS costs before resizing indexers.

Finance partners: durable buffers cost money on disk and object storage; queue_util trends belong in quarterly capacity decks alongside indexer counts.

Security partners: backlog can delay detection narratives; tie severity to tier and customer contracts using pipeline_inventory.csv rather than flat thresholds.

Audit partners: archive weekly CSV exports of CRITICAL and HIGH rows with ticket identifiers whenever deploy_freeze_until metadata changes.

Capacity partners: forecast indexer cores using trailing queue_util maxima per tier, not average midday samples.

SRE partners: integrate PagerDuty routes with team and owner columns so pages reach the correct rotation without manual lookup.

Executive partners: translate lag_s into minutes of blind spot for customer promises, not raw seconds alone.

Documentation partners: hyperlink runbooks from recommended_action tokens to avoid stale prose inside SPL.

Tooling partners: optional Splunk ITSI episodes can consume the same CSV exports when entities map cleanly to pipeline_name.

Long-term architecture: split golden path canaries onto dedicated tokens and topics so MEDIUM severity truly means controlled drift.

Hybrid cloud partners: document cross-region collector fan-out to explain lag without assuming single-site math.

Finally, human judgment still validates automated severity during customer-impacting incidents; this search guides investigations, not replaces incident command decisions.
grandmaExplanation: We measure how long it takes for diary entries from your systems to show up in the library where everyone reads them. When the mail trucks, sorting rooms, or librarians fall behind, we raise a clear signal before anyone assumes the story simply stopped happening.
hardware: Applies to Linux and Kubernetes-hosted log pipelines, Kafka clusters, Splunk Enterprise indexer and heavy forwarder tiers at 9.x or 10.x, Splunk Cloud stacks, AWS and Azure landing zones with object storage, and hybrid estates running Splunk Distribution of OpenTelemetry Collector alongside Fluentd, Fluent Bit, Filebeat, or Vector agents.
